simple_explanation: The Sponge Cake: The main body of the cake; it uses the most ingredients (data) but has no specific flavor yet.
The Icing: The specific flavor added on top. It requires precise handling but is smaller in volume than the sponge.
The Cherry on Top: The finishing touch that perfects the cake based on feedback, but can't be the whole meal.
The Ingredients: Flour, sugar, eggs. Bad ingredients (bad data) make a bad cake, no matter how good the chef is.
The Recipe: Step-by-step instructions. If the recipe is wrong, the cake fails even with good ingredients.
The Oven: You need heat and time to bake the batter. You cannot 'un-bake' it once it's done.
Tasting: Checking a slice to see if it's good. Averages might look good, but one slice could still be burnt.
Selling the Cake: Packaging it and giving it to customers.
Seasoning: Adjusting the amount of sugar or salt until the taste is perfect.
Oven Settings: Setting the temperature and timer before you put the cake in.
The Engine Block: A powerful V8 engine that can be put into a truck, car, or boat depending on how it's tuned.
The Steering Wheel: It directs where the massive engine power should go.
Fuel Drops: The engine processes fuel drop-by-drop to keep running.
Windshield View: How much of the road you can see at once. A small windshield means you miss the upcoming turn.
Tuning the Car: Adding a spoiler and turbocharger to a factory car to win a specific race.
Driving: You aren't building the car anymore; you're just driving it to your destination.
GPS Glitch: The map confidently tells you to turn left into a lake.
Suspension: Low temp is a stiff, predictable ride. High temp is a loose, bouncy, creative ride.
Turbo Lag: The delay between stepping on the gas and feeling the car accelerate.
Pit Stop: Saving your progress so if the car breaks down, you don't have to start the race from the beginning.
Traffic Network: Cars (data) flow through roads. If one road is blocked, traffic finds a new route.
The Student: Listens to a whisper, decides if it's important, and passes it on.
Grade Level: 1st grade learns letters, 5th grade learns sentences, 12th grade learns essays.
The Bouncer: Checks your ID. If the signal isn't strong enough, you don't get in.
The Blame Game: The project failed, so the boss goes back down the chain to fix the mistake.
Memorizing the Textbook: You know the exact words but fail the test because the questions changed slightly.
Skimming: You only read the chapter titles and can't answer any detailed questions.
Tinted Glasses: If you wear red glasses, the whole world looks red, even if it isn't.
Improvising: Playing a song you've never heard before just by knowing music theory.
Test Drive: Driving a car around the block twice to get the feel of it.
Show Your Work: Writing down the math steps, not just the final answer.
Open Book Exam: You can look up facts in the textbook to make sure you're right.
Forger vs Detective: One makes fake paintings, the other spots them. Both get better by competing.
Traffic Control: Watching every car at once to prevent accidents.
The Scanner: Turns a paper document into digital code.
The Printer: Turns digital code back into a readable picture.
Target Practice: How many arrows actually hit the bullseye?
Cooking Recipe: Follow these exact steps to get the result.
Sticky Notes: Putting tags on items so you know what they are.
Security Guard: Spotting the one person running in a crowd of walkers.
The Waiter: Takes your order to the kitchen and brings the food back.
The Universal Genius: A machine that can learn to do anything a human can do.
Autopilot: The plane flies itself while the pilot drinks coffee.
Word Soup: Throwing all words into a bowl without caring about grammar.
Laundry Load: Washing a pile of clothes together instead of one sock at a time.
Weather Forecast: Predicting rain based on clouds, humidity, and wind.
The Ocean: You can't drink it; you need a ship to navigate it.
Coin Toss: It's either Heads or Tails, nothing else.
Magician's Hat: You see the rabbit go in and come out, but don't know how.
Digital Receptionist: Answers common questions at the front desk.
Sorting Mail: Putting letters into the right zip code bins.
Renting Tools: Using a giant crane from a rental company instead of buying one.
Sorting Laundry: Whites in one pile, colors in another.
Digital Eyes: Teaching a camera to recognize a face.
Scoreboard: Shows hits, misses, and false alarms.
The Library: The entire collection of books the AI reads.
Smoke and Fire: Where there's smoke, there's usually fire.
Rotation: Testing a player in different positions to see if they are actually good.
Photocopying: Flipping and rotating a photo to create 'new' data.
Gold Panning: Sifting dirt to find nuggets of value.
Detective Work: finding clues in the evidence.
20 Questions Game: Is it an animal? Yes. Does it bark? No.
Digital Mask: Putting a realistic mask on an actor.
Shadow Puppets: A 3D hand makes a simple 2D shadow.
Art Critic: Deciding if the painting is real or fake.
Reflexes: Pulling your hand from fire before your brain even thinks about it.
Barcode: A number that represents a complex product.
Teamwork: A doctor, nurse, and surgeon working together is better than one alone.
Gym Reps: Doing a full set of exercises to build muscle.
Juicing: Getting the liquid out and leaving the peel behind.
Identity: Height, weight, and eye color are your features.
One-Way Street: Traffic only moves forward, never back.
Report Card: The average grade of your Math and English scores.
Rotten Cooking: If you use rotten eggs, the cake will be bad.
Street Smarts: Using what you learned in school in the real world.
The Artist: Painting a new picture, not just taking a photo.
Survival of the Fittest: Only the best solutions 'survive' to the next round.
Construction Crew: A thousand workers building a wall together (fast).
Hill Slope: The steepness of the hill you are climbing down.
Answer Key: The correct answers to the test.
Shortcut: Taking the back roads to avoid traffic.
The Kitchen: Customers eat the food, but the cooking happens here, unseen.
FaceID: Your phone recognizing it's you.
Front Door: Where guests enter the building.
Nervous System: Connecting all appliances to a central brain.
Baby Step: Taking one small step towards the destination.
Seating Chart: Arranging wedding guests so similar people sit together.
Peer Pressure: You act like the 5 people closest to you.
Mind Map: Drawing lines between related ideas.
Price Tag: Tells you what the item is worth.
Encyclopedia: Knows a little bit about everything.
Steering Sensitivity: Turn the wheel too much and you crash; too little and you don't turn.
Trend Line: Drawing a line through dots on a graph.
Pass/Fail: Predicting if you will pass the exam based on study hours.
Golf Score: The lower the score, the better you did.
Apprentice: Watching the master and learning to do it yourself.
Babel Fish: Instantly understanding a foreign language.
Library Card: Tells you where the book is, not the story itself.
The Brain: The final product that can think/predict.
Swiss Army Knife: Can cut, saw, and screw via one tool.
Stereotyping: Assuming it's a duck because it quacks.
Ghostwriter: Writing a speech for you.
Universal Translator: Understanding human speech.
Intersection: Where two roads meet.
Static: The fuzz on the radio that makes music hard to hear.
Currency Converter: Turning Dollars and Yen into Gold so they can be compared.
Where's Waldo: Finding the specific person in a crowd.
Switchboard: Turning on one specific light for each category.
Tuning: Adjusting a guitar string until it hits the perfect note.
Black Sheep: The one item that doesn't fit the group.
Checkout Counter: Where you get your receipt.
Knob: A control dial on a machine.
Cloud Gazing: Seeing shapes in the clouds.
Light Switch: It's either on or off.
Squinting: Blurring your eyes to see the big picture, not details.
Needle in Haystack: Finding only the needle, not the hay.
Weather Report: Guessing rain based on clouds.
Washing Veggies: Cleaning dirt before cooking.
College Graduate: Knows basics, needs job training.
The Jury: Ideally, 12 people decide better than 1.
Fishing Net: Catching ALL the fish, even if you catch some boots.
Personal Shopper: Suggesting clothes you might like.
Diary: Remembering what happened yesterday to understand today.
Crystal Ball: Predicting the exact stock price.
Speed Bump: Slowing down the car so it doesn't crash.
Gatekeeper: Only lets positive energy through.
The Body: The hardware that moves, controlled by the AI brain.
Off-Road Tires: Works well even on bumpy roads.
Scorecard: How far off were your guesses?
Elastic Band: Stretches to fit big or small items.
Color by Number: Coloring each section a specific color.
Highlighting: Marking important words in a textbook.
Fill in the Blank: Learning by guessing the missing word.
Reading Between the Lines: Understanding the mood, not just words.
Mood Ring: Detecting if the text is happy or sad.
Translation: English sentence in, French sentence out.
Dimmer Switch: Smoothly transitioning from off to on.
Pie Chart: Showing percentages that add up to 100%.
Stenographer: Typing what is spoken.
Excel Sheet: Rows and columns.
Hive Mind: Many brains working as one.
The Fence: Drawing a line to separate sheep from goats.
Simulation: Video game world used for training.
Final Exam: Checking if the student learned.
Audiobook: Turning text into voice.
Stock Chart: Prices changing over time.
Chopping: Cutting veggies into bite-sized pieces.
Specialist: A worker who only does one job, but extremely fast.
Practice: Rehearsing before the big show.
Renaissance Man: Using math skills to learn coding.
Blind Test: Can you tell if it's Coke or Pepsi?
Junk Drawer: Random items mixed together.
Pop Quiz: Checking progress before the final.
Jitter: Shaking hands when trying to aim.
Arrow: Pointing in a specific direction with force.
Butler: Serves your requests.
Dreaming: Being in a world that isn't there.
Tour Guide: Looking at a statue and explaining it.
Calculator: Good at math, bad at poetry.
Muscle: Stronger weights mean stronger pull.
Map Coordinates: Every word has a location on the map.
Glass Box: Seeing how the gears turn inside.
Intuition: Guessing right without studying.
Used in generative AI to define a model that adjusts learning processes based on feedback it receives. Adaptive learning models enhance their ability to generate accurate outputs.
Inputs deliberately designed to confuse or tick a generative AI model into producing incorrect outputs. They are often indiscernible to the human eye.

Often referred to as Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). That means two neural networks (a generator and a discriminator) are trained simultaneously and competitively to improve the quality of generated outputs.
Systematic errors in an algorithm that generates unfair outcomes, often privileging one group of users over another.
Describes the capability of generative AI systems to create art, music, literature, or other creative works without direct human input.

Defined rules that enable applications to communicate with each other. It acts as an intermediary layer that processes data transfers between systems, letting companies open their application data and functionality to third parties.
The simulation of human intelligence in machines that are programmed to think like humans. Check out: An ultimate guide to artificial intelligence.
A technique used in generative models, especially language generation, which helps focus on parts of input date, improving the relevance and coherence of generated content.
A neural network used in generative AI that is trained to encode input data as representations, then decode representations back to the original data.
Using ML, NLP, and alternative AI-guided techniques to classify text in an increasingly fast, cost-effective, and accurate manner.
The ability of a generative AI system to create content or make decisions independently, without specific guidance or rules set by humans. B

A supervised learning method of training neural networks. It works by standardising inputs to a network, which stabilises learning, saves time, and improves accuracy. Batch normalisation can be quite complex, so for more details check out: Batch normalisation guide.

A transformer-based ML technique for NLP. BERT is often the baseline of NLP experiments after being introduced in 2018 by researchers at Google. BERT was pretrained on language modelling and next sentence prediction.

The technique of combining features or aspects from multiple sources or datasets to create new, hybrid outputs. Typically used in generative AI, blending can lead to deeper and more complex forms of analyses. Altair provide a great explanation: What is Data Blending? C
Categorisation is an NLP function that assigns a category to a document.

The combined application of AI techniques to broaden the level of knowledge representations and to solve a wider range of problems in a more efficient manner. Check out Gartner’s helpful definition for more detail: Composite AI.

The study and development of AI systems that can perform tasks traditionally considered to require creativity, such as composing music, painting, writing poetry, and so on. The field encompasses both the theoretical and practical aspects of creativity in AI.

Computational linguistics is an interdisciplinary field concerned with the computational modelling of natural language. Computational linguists apply skills to develop and analyse computer applications of translation, voice recognition, text analysis, and so on.

Computational semantics studies the automation of the construction and reasoning of meaning representations of natural language expressions. Semantics is concerned with computing the meaning of linguistic objects and combines insights from computational linguistics.
A process in generative AI where the model generates data based on certain conditions or parameters, such as generating images of a specific category.
The use of AI, particularly generative models, to create content, including text, images, music, and videos. For a guide, check out: How to master generative AI content.
The representation of words or features in the context of their surrounding words or features, improving the relevance and coherence of the generated content.
Used by developers to build conversational user interfaces, chatbots, and virtual assistants. They offer integration into chat interfaces such as messaging platforms, SMS, and websites.
A type of deep neural network commonly used in generative AI, especially for image generation and analysis. CNNs can detect patterns and features in images.
Measures used to assess the novelty, quality, and diversity of the content produced by AI models.
A training strategy in generative AI where models are gradually exposed to increasingly complex data, improving their ability to generate complex outputs.
A concept used in Generative Adversarial Networks, particularly in tasks like image-to-image translation. It ensures that the original input can be recovered after a series of transformations. D
Values that convey information, such as quantity, quality, facts, or other meaning. For more information, and to understand the analysis of data, check out: Data analytics 101.
A collection of data derived from a single source or collected for a single project.

Videos in which the face and/or voice of a person, most commonly a public figure, has been manipulated by AI software in a way that makes the video seem authentic. See: What are deepfakes and how to protect against them.
A subset of machine learning involving neural networks with many layers. DL is integral to generative AI models, especially in processing complex inputs like images and natural language.
A technique in generative AI where the model learns to remove noise or imperfections from data, often used in image and audio processing.
DYM is an natural language processing function [JS1] used in search applications to identify typos in a query or suggest similar queries that could produce results in the search database being used.
A digital twin uses real world data to create simulations that can predict how a product or process will perform.
A representation where individual dimensions capture single, independent factors of variation in the data, aiding in more controlled generation.
A technique in generative AI where the model learns to generate data that matches the distribution of the training dataset, ensuring realistic and diverse output.
The ability of a generative model to adapt to new, unseen domains of data, maintaining performance even when the new data differs from the training data.
An algorithm in generative AI for measuring similarity between two temporal sequences, which may vary in speed. It is particularly useful in speech recognition. E

A type of recurrent neural network where connections between units form a directed cycle. The architecture is useful in generative models for tasks that involve sequential data, like language modelling.
The process of protecting information or data by using mathematical models to scramble it in such a way that only the parties who have the key to unscramble it can gain access.
The measure of randomness or uncertainty in the model’s predictions. It is a key concept in understanding and controlling the diversity of generated content.

A subset of AI algorithms inspired by the process of natural selection. These algorithms iteratively select, mutate, and breed candidate solutions. They are sometimes used in generative AI to evolve novel solutions to problems.

Branch of AI focused on making the decision-making processes of AI systems transparent and understandable to humans. Explainable AI is becoming more important in generative AI models, as people require trust and transparency. For more, see: The importance of ethics.
The generative AI model’s ability to generate outputs or make predictions for data points that lie outside the range of its training data. F
A type of neural network where connections between nodes do not form cycles. The architecture is commonly used in simpler generative models.

In evolutionary algorithms, a fitness function quantitatively evaluates how close a given design solution is to achieving the set aims. In generative AI, this concept can be used to guide the generation process towards desired outcomes.
The process in neural networks, including generative models, in which inputs are passed forward through the network layers to generate an output.
The use of generative AI to create individual frames in video and animation, often for the purpose of creating smooth transitions or filling in missing frames.

A form of many-valued logic where truth values can be any real number between 0 and 1, representing degrees of truth. In generative AI, it is used to handle imprecise or uncertain information. For more information, check out: A very brief introduction to Fuzzy Logic and Fuzzy Systems G
Neural network architecture used in unsupervised machine learning, involving two parts: the generator, which generates data, and the discriminator, which evaluates it.
A type of language processing AI model designed to generate human-like text. It is perhaps the most prominent example of a generative AI model.
A type of neural network which directly runs on the graph structure. GNNs are used in generative models that deal with graph-structured data.
Tech that simulates the sense of touch through force, vibrations, or motions to the user. Often used in generative AI to create virtual environments or to enhance user interactions.
Converts input data into a fixed-size string of bytes, typically used for data retrieval. Hashing can play a role in generative AI for data indexing or ensuring the uniqueness of generated content.
A theory of how brain neurons adapt during learning process, often summarised as “cells that fire together wire together.” Inspires approaches and algorithms in neural network training.
A mathematical optimisation technique that starts with an arbitrary solution to a problem and iteratively makes minor changes to the solution, each improving it a bit more.
In machine learning, including generative AI, a hyperplane is a decision boundary that helps to classify data points. It is especially relevant in models that involve classification tasks. I
A process in generative AI where the model transforms an input image into an output image, keeping the core structure but altering its style or specific characteristics.
Situations in machine learning and generative AI where some classes of data are much more frequent than others. Handling imbalanced data is crucial for training effective generative models.
A method used in deep learning and generative AI to find a compact representation of the input data that preserves the most relevant information about the output task.

An autonomous entity in AI that sees and acts upon an environment and directs its activity towards achieving goals. In generative AI, such agents can be designed to generate responses or actions based on environmental inputs. For more: What is an intelligent agent?
When an AI model memorises training examples and uses them to make predictions. The approach can be utilised in generative AI for applications where examples resemble test cases.
The creation of a one-to-one correspondence between elements of two sets, which can be used in tasks like graph generation and structure-preserving transformations.
In the context of generative AI development, refers to testing where individual units or components of a model are combined and tested as a group to evaluate their interactions.
AI model generating data that falls within the range of its training dataset. Opposed to extrapolation where the model generates data outside the range of its training data.
An approach where the model gradually improves over time, often by repeatedly processing data and refining algorithms. Common method for generative models. J
Measures similarity between two probability distributions. Used in generative AI to analyse difference between the generated data distribution and real data distribution.
A technique used in data augmentation where small, random perturbations are added to data (like images or audio) to create a more robust dataset for training generative models.
The probability distribution over all combinations of values in a set of variables. Important for generating data that captures the relationships between variables.
A training approach where a model is trained to perform multiple tasks simultaneously, which can lead to more generalised and robust generative models.

Also known as skip connections, these are connections in a neural network that skip one or more layers. In generative models, they help in preserving information and mitigating the vanishing gradient problem.

Part of Project Jupyter, Jupyter notebook is an open-source web application that allows the creation and sharing of documents containing live code, equations, visualisations, and narrative text. It is widely used in generative AI for experimentation, data analysis, and model development. See more: How to Use Jupyter Notebooks: The Ultimate Guide.
The compilation of certain parts of the code during runtime, which can optimise the performance of AI models, particularly in terms of speed and resource usage. K
A method of vector quantisation used for cluster analysis in data mining, aiming to partition data points into clusters with similar characteristics.
A simple, non-parametric algorithm used for classification and regression in machine learning, often applied in generative AI for tasks like anomaly detection.

A high-level neural networks application programming interface in Python, used for prototyping deep learning models, including generative models like Generative Adversarial Networks and Variational Autoencoders (VAEs).
In machine learning, a kernel is a function used to transform data into a higher-dimensional space, enabling better classification when data is not linearly separable in the original space.
In animation and film, a keyframe defines the starting and ending points of a smooth transition. Keyframes can be used in generative AI for animation and frame interpolation.
A measure of divergence between probability distributions, often used in generative AI to quantify the difference between generated and training data distributions.
A collection of data, information, and rules used for knowledge management. Generative AI can utilise knowledge bases to generate contextually relevant outputs.
The process of extracting valuable knowledge from large datasets, a technique used in generative AI to improve understanding and generation capabilities.
The way in which intelligent agents, like generative models, represent knowledge about the world to make decisions or generate content. L
A regularisation technique used in training generative AI models to prevent overconfidence in predictions by penalising overly confident outputs, encouraging more diverse and calibrated predictions.
The process of using generative AI to create human-like text or speech, often employed in chatbots, virtual assistants, and in content generation.

Type of AI algorithm that uses deep learning techniques and massively large data sets to understand, summarise, generate, and predict new content. For more information, check out: What’s a large language model?
The abstract, high-dimensional space in which the model represents data. It is essential in models like Generative Adversarial Networks and variational autoencoders.
A technique used to normalise activations in neural network layers. It can improve the stability and training speed of generative models.
These models combine generative AI techniques with reinforcement learning, enabling the generation of sequences with specific desired characteristics.
A measure of how well a generative model’s predictions match the actual data distribution. It is used to assess the quality of generated content.
A mathematical function used in training generative AI models to quantify the difference between the model’s predictions and the actual data. It guides the model towards better performance.
Recurrent neural network architecture well-suited for sequential data, making it valuable in tasks like text generation, language modelling, and speech recognition. M

Use and development of computer systems able to learn and adapt without following explicit instructions, by using algorithms and statistical models to analyse and draw inferences from patterns in data. For more information, check out: What is Machine Learning?
A stochastic model describing a sequence of events where the probability of each event depends only on the state attained in the previous event. Markov chains are used in generative models.

Neural network architectures that incorporate external memory modules, allowing them to store and access information over extended sequences. These networks are valuable in generative tasks with long-term dependencies.
A machine learning technique where a model learns how to learn. In generative AI, meta-learning can help models adapt quickly to new tasks or generate content with diverse styles.
A subset of data used during training in generative AI. Minibatch training is an efficient way to update model parameters and manage large datasets.
A neural network architecture used for modelling probability distributions over continuous variables. MDNs are employed in generative AI to manage uncertainty and generate probabilistic outputs.
A technology used to record the movement of objects or humans. In generative AI, motion capture data can be used to create realistic animations and simulations.
A technique in which multiple generative AI models are combined to improve performance. Ensemble methods can enhance the diversity and quality of generated content.
The generation of content that involves multiple modes of input or output, such as generating both text and images together. Multimodal generative AI models are used in tasks like image captioning.
An approach that configures a prompt representing a variable — that can be changed — to allow repetitive prompts where only the variable changes. N
A field of AI focussing on the interaction between computers and human language, enabling generative AI models to understand, generate, and manipulate natural language text.
The ability of generative AI models to comprehend and interpret human language, enabling them to generate contextually relevant responses and content.
Solutions that automatically convert structured data, such as that found in a database, an application, or a live feed, into a text-based narrative.
A technique used in training generative AI models to sample negative examples, improving their ability to distinguish between positive and negative instances.
A process in generative AI where data points are generated in the vicinity of existing data points, often used for data augmentation, and enhancing model robustness.
A method in which generative AI models are used to automatically search for the optimal neural network architecture for a given task.
A framework that uses neural networks to solve differential equations.
A technique in generative AI that combines the content of one image with the artistic style of another, producing visually appealing images that merge content and style.
A type of computing inspired by the structure and function of the human brain. In generative AI, neuromorphic computing can lead to more efficient and brain-like AI systems.
Data that contains errors, outliers, or irrelevant information. Managing noisy data is essential in generative AI to ensure the quality of generated content.
Generative AI models that do not rely on the Markov property, allowing them to capture more complex dependencies between data points. O
A mathematical function that the generative AI model aims to optimise. It quantifies the model’s performance and guides it towards better generation.
A formal representation of knowledge or concepts and their relationships in a specific domain. Generative AI can use ontologies to generate more contextually relevant content.

A research organisation and company known for developing innovative AI models, including GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) models. OpenAI has made significant contributions to the field of generative AI.
Tech used in generative AI to convert printed or handwritten text into machine-readable text, allowing models to generate or process textual data from images.
An oracle model is a hypothetical model that has access to perfect information or an idealised version of the training data. It serves as a benchmark for evaluating generative models.
Technique in generative AI used to identify data points that are different from the training data. OOD detection helps improve the reliability of generative models.
A strategy in deep learning where a generative AI has more parameters than necessary. It can lead to improved model performance but may require careful handling to prevent overfitting. P
A method of performing multiple computations simultaneously, used in generative AI to speed up training and inference processes by leveraging multiple processors or graphics processing units.
In generative AI for translation and language tasks, parallel data consists of text or speech in one language and its corresponding translation or interpretation in another language.
A neural network component in generative AI models that processes sensory inputs, such as images or audio, and extracts relevant features for further processing or generation.
A type of generative AI model that generates images pixel-by-pixel, considering the dependencies between pixels in the image.
A class of reinforcement learning algorithms used to train models to make sequential decisions and generate content, often employed in tasks like game playing and robotics.
A dimensionality reduction technique used in generative AI for data compression and feature extraction, particularly in image and video generation.
A framework that represents probabilistic relationships between variables using graphs, commonly used in generative AI for modelling complex dependencies.
An improvement of the Generative Adversarial Network architecture that gradually increases the resolution of generated images during training, resulting in high-quality images.
A technique in generative AI model optimisation that involves removing unnecessary or low-impact neural network connections or parameters to reduce model size and improve efficiency. Q
Quality assessment in generative AI involves evaluating the quality of generated content, such as images, text, or audio, to ensure that it meets certain criteria and standards.
The process of mapping continuous values to a discrete set of values. Often used in tasks like image compression, where continuous colour values are mapped to a limited palette of colours.
A technique used in information retrieval and generative AI to improve search results. Involves expanding queries with related terms or synonyms to retrieve relevant information.
A publicly available dataset containing millions of hand-drawn sketches contributed by users. Often used in for tasks like sketch generation and recognition. R
A technique that considers both user preferences and item properties in recommendation systems, enhancing the quality of recommendations.
A system that provides personalised recommendations to users, commonly used in applications like movie recommendations and product suggestions.
A type of generative AI model that incorporates recurrent layers to generate sequences of data, making it suitable for tasks like text generation and music composition.
A type of neural network architecture used in generative AI for processing sequential data, making it suitable for tasks like text generation and speech recognition.
A machine learning paradigm where agents learn to make decisions through interactions with an environment. Used in generative AI for tasks like game playing and robotic control.
The process of learning meaningful representations of data that can be used for generative AI tasks. It involves capturing essential features from raw data.
A deep neural network architecture known for its skip connections, allowing it to train very deep models. ResNet is used in generative AI for various computer vision tasks.
A generative AI model used in unsupervised learning for feature learning and data representation. RBMs are building blocks in deep belief networks.

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is an AI technique for improving the quality of LLM-generated responses by including trusted sources of knowledge, outside of the original training set, to improve the accuracy of the large language model’s output.

A key component in transformers, a type of neural network architecture used in generative AI, which enables models to weigh the importance of distinct parts of input sequences when generating content.
A computer vision task in generative AI where models classify each pixel in an image into a specific category, enabling tasks like object recognition and scene understanding.
In generative AI, these are fixed-length representations of sentences, often used to capture semantic information for various natural language processing tasks.
An optimisation algorithm used in training generative AI models that iteratively adjusts model parameters to minimise a loss function.
A generative AI technique that involves altering the style of an input, such as converting a photograph into the style of a famous artist’s painting.
A generative AI model known for its ability to generate high-quality images with fine-grained control over style attributes.

SAO is an natural language processing function that identifies the logical function of portions of sentences in terms of the elements that are acting as the subject of an action, the action itself, the object receiving the action (if one exists), and any adjuncts if present.

A symbolic approach designs a system using specific, narrow instructions that guarantee the recognition of a linguistic pattern. Rule-based solutions tend to have a high degree of precision, though they may require more work than machine learning-based solutions to cover the entire scope of a problem, depending on the application.
Generative AI models designed to oversee sequential data with a temporal aspect, such as time series forecasting and video generation.
An open-source machine learning framework developed by Google that is widely used in generative AI for model development and training.
A generative AI task where models generate human-readable text based on input data or prompts. Text generation is used in applications like chatbots, content generation, and language translation.
A technology in generative AI that converts text into spoken language, enabling the generation of natural-sounding speech from written text.
A unit of content corresponding to a subset of a word. Tokens are processed internally by large language models and can also be used as metrics for usage and billing.
The process of splitting text into individual units, or tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters. Tokenisation is a crucial step in generative AI for text processing.
A generative AI technique used to identify topics or themes within a collection of documents. It is commonly applied in tasks like document clustering and content recommendation.
A text generation strategy in generative AI where the model selects from the top-k next tokens at each step, controlling the diversity of generated text.

A text generation strategy in generative AI where the model selects from a subset of the tokens with cumulative probability less than or equal to a threshold “p.” This strategy also controls text diversity.

Display copious amounts of hierarchically structured (tree-structured) data. The space in the visualisation is split up into rectangles that are sized and ordered by a quantitative variable. The levels in the hierarchy of the treemap are visualised as rectangles containing other rectangles.
The process of quantifying the uncertainty associated with model predictions. It is crucial for understanding the reliability of generated content.
A mathematical concept stating that neural networks with enough parameters can approximate any continuous function.
A technique in generative AI that involves increasing the resolution or size of data, such as images or audio, often used in tasks like image generation and super-resolution. V
A model combining elements of autoencoders and variational inference to learn probabilistic representations of data, often used for generating new samples.
A technique used to discretise continuous data, commonly applied in tasks like speech and image compression.
The use of generative AI to create immersive and interactive virtual environments, enabling experiences in virtual reality.
A task where models answer questions about images or visual data, requiring both vision and language understanding.
A technology in generative AI that allows for the replication of a person’s voice, often used in applications like voice assistants and voice synthesis. W
WaveGAN is a generative AI model designed for generating audio waveforms, particularly in tasks like speech synthesis and music generation.
A type of learning in generative AI where models are trained with limited or noisy supervision, often used when obtaining precise labels or annotations is challenging.
The process of setting initial values for neural network weights. Proper weight initialisation can significantly impact the training and performance of generative AI models.

A method that uses a portion of a document as metacontext or metacontent. Word2Vec: A technique used to learn word embeddings from large text corpora. It is commonly used for capturing semantic similarities between words.
Vector representations of words that capture semantic relationships between words in a continuous vector space. They are essential for various natural language processing tasks. X
A weight initialisation technique in generative AI that helps set initial weights in neural networks to ensure efficient training and improved model convergence.
XML generation involves creating structured data in XML format from various sources, such as text or databases, for tasks like document generation and data interchange.

A classic problem in machine learning and generative AI where models need to learn to perform an exclusive OR operation on binary inputs, often used to evaluate the capabilities of neural networks. Y YAML ain’t markup language (YAML): YAML is a human-readable data serialisation format used in generative AI and other fields to represent data structures in a readable and concise manner.
The process of producing or generating output, often used in the context of code or functions that generate data or results.
YOLO is a real-time object detection algorithm in generative AI that can detect and locate multiple objects within images or video frames with high accuracy and speed.
A probability distribution in generative AI to model distribution of number of attempts required to observe a new distinct event, often used in certain text generation models. Z
A statistical measure used in generative AI for data normalisation and outlier detection by measuring how many standard deviations a data point is from the mean.
A statistical model used in generative AI to account for data with an excess of zero values, often applied in count data and rare event prediction.
An advanced generative AI task where models are trained to generate images of objects or concepts they have never seen during training.
Machines designed to imitate human cognitive functions like learning, problem-solving, and decision-making.
A subset of machine learning that uses neural networks with multiple layers (deep neural networks) to learn and make decisions.
A technique in neural networks that allows the model to focus on specific parts of the input when producing an output.
The goal of creating machines that can learn and reason like humans across various tasks.
Large AI models trained on vast amounts of data, serving as a base for various applications.
AI models trained on enormous text datasets to understand and generate human-like text.
Compact AI models designed for specific language tasks, often used on devices with limited resources.
AI systems trained to understand and analyze visual information from images or videos.
AI systems that can process and understand multiple types of information, such as text, images, and sound.
AI models that convert words, sentences, or images into numerical representations for easier processing.
The art of crafting effective instructions or questions to get the best results from an AI model.
Harmful, offensive, or biased content generated by an AI system.
The initial training of an AI model on a large dataset before fine-tuning for specific tasks.
An AI system designed to perform actions or make decisions autonomously to achieve specific goals.
A machine learning technique that trains algorithms across multiple decentralized devices or servers holding local data samples, without exchanging them.
A machine is described as autonomous if it can perform its task or tasks without needing human intervention.
A method where the model starts with the desired output and works in reverse to find data that might support it.
Commonly used in image or video tagging, this is an imaginary box drawn on visual information to help a model recognize it as a distinct type of object.
A field within artificial intelligence that is primarily concerned with creating and analyzing machine learning algorithms.
A large dataset of written or spoken material that can be used to train a machine to perform linguistic tasks.
An umbrella term referring to the process of adding structure to data so that a machine can read it.
A method in which a machine must work from a problem to find a potential solution.
AI that could successfully do any intellectual task that can be done by any human being.
Values that affect the way your models learns. Occasionally used interchangeably with parameter, although the terms have some subtle differences.
Commonly used in training data for chatbots and other natural language processing tasks, this is a type of label that defines the purpose or goal of what is said.
A part of training data that identifies the desired output for that particular piece of data.
An umbrella term for various types of learning algorithms, including machine learning and deep learning.
This subset of AI is particularly focused on developing algorithms that will help machines to learn and change in response to new data, without the help of a human being.
This refers to the process by which a machine turns structured data into text or speech that humans can understand.
A method of teaching AI that sets a goal without specific metrics, encouraging the model to test different scenarios rather than find a single answer.
The process of identifying and categorizing opinions in a piece of text, often with the goal of determining the writer’s attitude towards something.
This field of research is focused on developing AI that is equal to the human mind when it comes to ability.
The unlabeled data used to check that a machine learning model is able to perform its assigned task.

Structured like training data with an input and labels, this data is used to test a recently trained model against new data and to analyze performance, with a particular focus on checking for overfitting.
Also called queries or utterances, these work in tandem with intents for natural language processing. The variation is what a person might say to achieve a certain purpose or goal.
like the engine block, providing the core power and functionality needed for the car to run. Without it, the car won’t move.
the process of using a trained AI model to make predictions or produce outputs based on new input data.
the input or instructions you give to an AI model to guide its behavior and output.
the internal settings of the AI model that determine how it processes input and generates output.
the settings you adjust during model inference to control how the model behaves.
tailoring a pre-trained model to better suit specific tasks or domains.
Stop sequences are a small but powerful tool in AI that keeps the system efficient, just as brakes are essential for a smooth driving experience
an electric brain that performs tasks like a human brain, but it's powered by computers.
a subset of AI that allows machines to improve at tasks based on experience.
subset of ML uses layers of neural networks to analyze various factors of data.
using a trained model to make predictions.
developing algorithms that make unbiased decisions, treating all groups equally.
how well a machine learning model generalizes to similar data to that on which it was trained.
deep learning models that understand and generate human-like text based on the training data they've been fed.
A technique for evaluating the importance of a feature or component by temporarily removing it from a model.
A statistical way of comparing two (or more) techniques—the A and the B.
A category of specialized hardware components designed to perform key computations needed for deep learning algorithms.
In reinforcement learning, the mechanism by which the agent transitions between states of the environment.
A training approach in which the algorithm chooses some of the data it learns from.
Synonym for tuning or fine-tuning.
A dynamic process in which an agent autonomously plans and executes actions to achieve a goal.
Output from a generative AI system that favors quantity over quality.
A mechanism used in a neural network that indicates the importance of a particular word or part of a word.
A technology that superimposes a computer-generated image on a user's view of the real world, thus providing a composite view.
Any automated process for building machine learning models.
A neural network containing more than one hidden layer.
Automatically making an association or assumption based on one's mind models and memories.
A mechanism by which data is loaded into a neural network.
A form of fine-tuning that improves a generative AI model's ability to follow instructions.
A model whose inputs, outputs, or both include more than one modality.
a type of neural network containing more than one hidden layer.
computing systems inspired by the human brain that are designed to recognize patterns and learn from data.
autonomous systems that perform tasks or make decisions based on their environment and user inputs, often without requiring direct human intervention.
AI language model that generates human-like text based on input.
a conversational AI assistant developed by Anthropic that focuses on creating helpful, safe, and aligned AI
a network of interconnected devices that collect and exchange data over the internet.
the process of teaching a machine learning model to recognize patterns and make predictions by feeding it data.
a real-time object detection system that can detect and classify multiple objects in an image with high accuracy.
A human-readable data serialization format often used for configuring machine learning experiments or storing structured data.
A technology that creates an immersive, simulated environment using AI and other tools
A neural network architecture commonly used for image segmentation tasks, particularly in medical imaging
A type of neural network architecture that uses two or more identical subnetworks to process different inputs and compare their outputs
An AI system that uses a set of predefined rules to make decisions or solve problems
A technique used to identify inputs that differ significantly from the training data, helping AI systems detect unusual or unexpected situations.
An AI technique used in human-robot interaction, where both the human and the robot focus on the same object or task
A neural network that operates on graph-structured data
A type of artificial neural network where connections between the nodes do not form cycles
A type of neural network designed to better understand spatial hierarchies in data
theoretical form of AI that can understand, learn, and apply knowledge across a wide range of tasks, much like a human.
converts spoken language into text, enabling applications like voice commands, transcription, and virtual assistants.
model developed by Google that treats every NLP task as a text generation problem
A type of deep learning algorithm that is particularly effective in image and video recognition tasks.
The process by which an AI system produces human-like text based on data input.
A machine or software that can perform tasks without human intervention.
A collaboration between humans and AI to enhance decision-making rather than replace human thinking.
A type of machine learning where an agent learns to make decisions by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards or penalties.
A set of tools and protocols that allow developers to integrate AI functionalities into applications.
AI agents with a physical body that perform specific tasks in the physical environment.
system that can handle input and produce output in several mediums.
an AI algorithm that is used to serve users content based on their preferences.
Robots are embodied mechanical machines that are capable of doing a physical task for humans.
We call things we don’t understand, “black boxes” because what happens inside the box cannot be seen.
a fine line between augmenting and replacing and technologies should be designed so that humans can choose what a system does and when it does it.
A  computer system or digital learning environment that gives instant and custom feedback to students.
AI can solve narrow problems
Images created with computers, increasingly enhanced by AI tools.
source: Beginner’s AI Glossary 2025: 55 Terms You Need to Know

simple_explanation: Learning from only a handful of examples, then generalising.

simple_explanation: Further training a pre-trained model on your specific data so it performs better on a particular task.

simple_explanation: A score that indicates how well a language model predicts text

simple_explanation: Humans rate model outputs so the model learns which answers are preferable.

simple_explanation: Converting audio speech into written words.

simple_explanation: Immersive, computer-generated environments experienced via a headset.
