simple_explanation: The Highlighter: Going through a text and highlighting specific names, places, or dates with different colors.
The Rephrasing: Asking 'Check balance' vs 'How much money do I have?'. Different words, same goal.
The Human Equal: A theoretical machine that can do any intellectual task a human can do, not just one specific job.
The Optical Illusion: Making tiny, invisible changes to an image that trick the AI into thinking a panda is a gibbon.
The Moral Compass: The set of rules and guidelines to ensure AI is used safely, fairly, and responsibly.
The Confident Lie: When an LLM generates false information but presents it as absolute fact.
The Drought: A period of reduced funding and interest in AI research due to overhyped expectations failing to materialize.
The Switch: A tiny mathematical unit that receives input, processes it, and passes it on, mimicking a brain cell.
The Focus: How far back in a sentence or document the model can 'look' while generating the current word.
The Self-Starter: An AI agent that can create its own to-do list and execute tasks without human prompting.
The Equalizer: Adjusting the inputs of each layer to keep them on a similar scale, making training faster and more stable.
The Scout: Exploring multiple possible future sentences at once and keeping only the most promising paths.
The Archer's Dilemma: Balancing between always missing the target in the same spot (bias) vs hitting randomly all over the board (variance).
The Memory Wipe: When an AI learns a new task but completely forgets how to do the old task.
The Matchmaker: An AI trained to understand how images and text descriptions relate to each other.
The Visual Cortex: A deep learning architecture specifically designed to process pixel data and recognize images.
The Rule of Law: Training AI to follow a set of high-level principles (a constitution) to ensure safety.
The Lifelong Learner: An AI system that constantly updates its knowledge as new data comes in.
The Pose Guide: An add-on for image generators that lets you control the structure (like a stick figure pose) of the final image.
The Finish Line: The point in training where the model stops improving because it has found the best possible solution.
The Wingman: An AI assistant that works alongside you in apps (like Word or VS Code) to suggest edits and content.
The Price Tag: A mathematical formula that measures how 'expensive' (wrong) the model's errors are.
The General Manager: The main chip in a computer that handles general tasks but is slow at heavy AI math.
The Uncertainty Penalty: A score that penalizes the model heavily when it is confidently wrong.
The Changing Times: When the data in the real world changes over time, making the old model obsolete.
The Dumping Ground: A massive storage repository that holds a vast amount of raw data in its native format.
The Sabotage: Deliberately feeding bad data into a model during training to corrupt its behavior.
The Border: The line drawn by the AI that separates one class (e.g., 'Cats') from another ('Dogs').
The Group Chat: A layer where every single neuron is connected to every single neuron in the next layer.
The Restoration: An image generator that starts with pure static (noise) and slowly refines it into a clear picture.
The Cliff Notes: Training a small student model to mimic the behavior of a massive teacher model.
The Sick Day: Randomly turning off neurons during training to force the network to be more robust.
The Quitting Time: Stopping training as soon as the model stops improving, to prevent overfitting.
The Local Brain: Running AI directly on your phone or device rather than sending data to the cloud.
The Map Coordinates: Turning words into lists of numbers so the computer can map their meaning.
The Surprise Skill: Capabilities that appear in large models that were not explicitly programmed (like learning to code).
The Grading Scale: The standard used to measure how good the model is (e.g., Accuracy, F1 Score).
The Rulebook: Old-school AI based on rigid If-Then rules written by human experts.
The Glass Box: Designing AI so humans can understand why it made a specific decision.
The Missed Alarm: When the model fails to spot something that is actually there.
The False Alarm: When the model flags something that isn't actually there.
The Prep Work: Manually selecting and transforming data variables to make them easier for the AI to learn.
The Refresher Course: Taking a pre-trained model and training it a little bit more on a specific topic.
The Forger and Detective: Two AIs competing—one creates fakes, the other tries to spot them.
The Streamlined Memory: A simpler, faster version of LSTM for processing sequences.
The Autocomplete: A powerful model architecture designed to predict the next word in a sequence.
The Workhorse: A computer chip originally for video games, now essential for the heavy math of AI training.
The Downhill Walk: The mathematical method used to find the lowest error by taking small steps down the slope.
The App Store: A popular hub where researchers share and download AI models.
The Supervisor: Keeping a human involved to check the AI's work or provide feedback.
The Knob Twisting: Experimenting with settings (like learning rate) to find the best configuration.
The Cookie Cutter: Separating an image into distinct objects by outlining their exact shapes.
The Standard Exam: A massive database of images used to benchmark how good a vision model is.
The Hint: Teaching the model a new task just by explaining it in the prompt, without changing its weights.
The Assumption: The built-in preferences a model has for certain types of solutions.
The Tagging: The manual process of identifying data (e.g., 'This is a cat') for the AI to learn.
The Glue: A software library that connects LLMs to other tools and data sources.
The Open Challenger: Meta's series of powerful open-source language models.
The Building Blocks: Basic physical circuits (AND, OR, NOT) that make up a computer processor.
The Raw Scores: The unnormalized numbers the model outputs before they are turned into probabilities.
The Sticky Note: Fine-tuning a model by adding a tiny layer of new info rather than rewriting the whole brain.
The Journal: A type of neural network that remembers past information for a long time, good for text.
The Fill-in-the-Blank: Training an AI by hiding words in a sentence and asking it to guess them.
The Art Generator: A popular AI tool that creates artistic images from text prompts.
The Assembly Line: The engineering discipline of deploying and maintaining AI models in the real world.
The Synapse Strength: The strength of the connection between two neurons, which changes as the AI learns.
The Sense: A type of data input, such as text, image, or audio.
The Specialist Team: A model architecture that routes different questions to different sub-models.
The Inertia: A technique that helps the optimizer keep moving in the right direction through small bumps.
The Committee: Letting the AI look at different parts of the sentence simultaneously to catch different meanings.
The Specialist: AI that is super-human at one task (like Chess) but can't do anything else.
The Hybrid: Combining deep learning (patterns) with symbolic AI (logic rules) for better reasoning.
The Curve: The ability of a model to understand complex relationships, not just straight lines.
The Text Reader: Turning a picture of a document into editable text.
The Quick Learner: Learning a new class from just a single example.
The Public Recipe: Software or models where the code is free for anyone to use and modify.
The Lite Update: Tuning only a small part of the model to save time and memory.
The Settings: The internal variables (weights) that the model learns during training.
The Jailbreak: Tricking an AI into ignoring its safety rules by hiding commands in the input.
The Research Lab: A popular code library for building deep learning models, loved by researchers.
The Rounding: Reducing the precision of the model's numbers to make it run on smaller devices.
The Super Future: Theoretical AI running on quantum computers for exponential speedups.
The Gate: A popular activation function that outputs zero for negative inputs and the raw value for positive ones.
The Coaching: Refining a model by having humans rate its answers as good or bad.
The Safety Check: Developing AI with fairness, accountability, and transparency in mind.
The Deviation: A standard way to measure the average error of a model's predictions.
The Selection: Choosing a subset of data or a next word from a probability distribution.
The Meaning Search: Searching for concepts, not just exact keywords.
The Helper: Using a small amount of labeled data to help learn from a huge amount of unlabeled data.
The Converter: A model architecture used for tasks like translation (Input Text -> Output Text).
The Smoother: An S-shaped curve that squashes numbers between 0 and 1, used for probabilities.
The Point of No Return: A hypothetical future moment when AI intelligence surpasses human control.
The Super Engine: The most powerful AI chip from NVIDIA, used to train massive models like GPT-4.
The Translator: A coding language that lets software talk directly to the graphics card (GPU) to run math fast.
The Math Specialist: A tiny part of a GPU designed specifically to multiply matrices (the math of AI) instantly.
The Desk Space: Video RAM. If you don't have enough, you can't open the big model (book) to read it.
The Delivery Truck: A specialized computer optimized just for answering user questions quickly.
The Brain Power: A raw measure of how many math problems a computer can solve in one second.
The Hive: A giant warehouse filled with thousands of servers where the AI 'lives'.
The Supercomputer: Connecting many computers to work together as one giant brain.
The Assembly Speed: How many words (tokens) the AI can generate per second.
The Pipe Size: How much data can move between the memory and the processor at once.
The Multimodal Expert: Google's AI that processes video, audio, and text natively from the start.
The Scratchpad: Asking the AI to write out its thinking steps before giving the final answer.
The Example Method: Showing the AI three examples of what you want before asking it to do it.
The Open Book Test: Letting the AI look up facts in a reliable database before answering.
The Lite Update: Fine-tuning a model by updating only a tiny fraction of its weights to save money.
The Whisper: Optimizing a prompt mathematically rather than using human words to get better results.
The Converter: A model architecture designed to translate one sequence (English) into another (French).
The App Store: The central website where researchers share and download open-source AI models.
The Next-Word Guesser: A model that generates text one word at a time, based on what it just wrote.
The Strategist: An executive role focused on how a company uses AI safely and profitably.
The Insight Hunter: Someone who uses math and code to find answers in data.
The Plumber: Someone who builds the systems that move and store data for the scientists.
The AI Whisperer: A role focused entirely on writing the best inputs to get good results from AI.
The Launch: Putting a trained model into a real app where people can use it.
The Secret Use: Employees using AI tools at work without their boss or IT department knowing.
The Open Champion: Meta's powerful open-source model.
The Helpful Colleague: An AI by Anthropic designed to be safer and reliable.
The Efficient French Chef: A highly efficient open-source model from Europe.
The Open Artist: An AI that generates images from text, which you can run on your own PC.
The Movie Director: An AI that creates realistic video clips from text.
The Listener: OpenAI's accurate speech-to-text model.
The Arena: A website where data scientists compete to build the best models.
The Time Machine: A tool for saving different versions of your code.
The Shipping Container: Packaging code so it runs exactly the same on any computer.
The Password: A code that lets your software talk to a paid service like OpenAI.
The Concept Store: A database designed to store and search meanings (embeddings) efficiently.
The Overlap Score: Measuring how similar two sets of data are by dividing their intersection by their union.
The Error Counter: Counting the fraction of labels that are incorrectly predicted.
The Trade-off Graph: A chart showing the performance of a classification model at all classification thresholds.
The Performance Score: A single number that summarizes the ROC curve; 1.0 is perfect, 0.5 is a random guess.
The Simple Average: The average of the absolute errors; easier to understand than MSE.
The Goodness of Fit: A statistical measure of how close the data are to the fitted regression line.
The Safety Margin: A range of values so defined that there is a specified probability that the value of a parameter lies within it.
The Evidence Score: A measure of the probability that an observed difference could have occurred just by random chance.
The Standardizer: A number describing how many standard deviations a data point is from the mean.
The Direction: A vector that doesn't change direction when a linear transformation is applied to it.
The City Block: Measuring distance between two points by summing the absolute differences of their coordinates.
The Flight Path: Measuring the straight-line distance between two points.
The Angle Gap: The opposite of cosine similarity; measuring how different the angles of two vectors are.
The Artist: OpenAI's advanced image generation model that follows complex instructions.
The Mastermind: OpenAI's most advanced multimodal model, capable of reasoning and coding.
The Smart Colleague: Anthropic's latest model family, known for low hallucination and high safety.
The Google Brain: The large language model underlying many of Google's AI services.
The Conversationalist: Google's breakthrough conversation technology.
The Giant: A massive transformer model developed by NVIDIA for research.
The Big Reader: A larger version of the original BERT model for better understanding.
The Optimized Reader: A robustly optimized approach to BERT pretraining.
The Lite Reader: A smaller, faster, cheaper version of BERT.
The Fast Eye: The latest version of the 'You Only Look Once' object detection system.
The Deep Vision: A specific 50-layer deep neural network used for image recognition.
The Classic Vision: An older but reliable deep learning architecture for image classification.
The Pioneer: The deep neural network that started the modern AI revolution in 2012.
The Chess King: The IBM computer that defeated Garry Kasparov.
The Jeopardy Winner: IBM's question-answering computer system.
The Biologist: DeepMind's AI that predicts 3D structures of proteins.
The Composer: Google's model for generating high-fidelity music from text.
The Plotter: A python library for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations.
The Beautifier: A python library based on Matplotlib that makes drawing attractive statistical graphics easier.
The Computer Eye: A huge open-source library for computer vision and image processing.
The Text Toolkit: The Natural Language Toolkit, a classic library for working with human language data.
The Text Speedster: An industrial-strength natural language processing library for Python.
The Demo Builder: A library for quickly creating web interfaces for your ML models.
The App Builder: Turning data scripts into shareable web apps in minutes.
The Server: A modern, fast web framework for building APIs with Python.
The Micro-Server: A lightweight web application framework.
The Database Language: The standard language for storing, manipulating, and retrieving data in databases.
The Flexible Database: Databases that store data in formats other than relational tables (like JSON).
The Document Store: A popular NoSQL database program.
The Big Data Engine: An analytics engine for large-scale data processing.
The Elephant: A framework that allows for the distributed processing of large data sets across clusters of computers.
The Captain: An open-source system for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.
The Dripping Tap: A variation of ReLU that allows a small, non-zero gradient when the unit is not active.
The S-Curve: An activation function that squashes numbers between -1 and 1.
The Adaptive Learner: An optimizer that adapts the learning rate to the parameters.
The Standard Choice: A method for efficient stochastic gradient descent that is very popular.
The Stabilizer: An optimization algorithm designed to handle the vanishing gradient problem.
The Balanced Start: A way of setting the initial weights of a network so signals don't vanish or explode.
The Robust Start: An initialization method specifically designed for layers using ReLU.
The Forget Percentage: The specific number (e.g., 0.5) determining how many neurons are turned off.
The Big Step: Moving the filter 2 pixels at a time, effectively shrinking the image size by half.
The Frame: Adding a border of zeros around the image to keep the output size the same.
The Highlight Reel: Taking the largest number from a grid to downsample the image.
The Blur: Taking the average number from a grid to downsample the image.
The First Big Cheque: The first significant round of venture capital financing.
The Starter Money: The very first money raised by a startup to prove the idea works.
The Stock Market Debut: When a private company offers shares to the public in a new stock issuance.
The Billion Dollar Club: When a startup is valued at over $1 billion.
The Ten Billion Club: A startup valued at over $10 billion (like OpenAI).
The Business Helper: AI tools sold to other companies (like Salesforce Einstein).
The Consumer Helper: AI tools sold to regular people (like ChatGPT).
The Rental: Software as a Service - paying a monthly fee to use software hosted in the cloud.
The Quit Rate: The percentage of customers who stop using your AI service.
The Loyalty Score: The percentage of customers who keep using your service.
The Price per User: How much money you spend on ads to get one new customer.
The User Worth: The total amount of money a customer is expected to spend with your company.
The Profit Check: Measuring the gain or loss generated on an investment relative to the amount of money invested.
The Copycat: Predicting a data point's label by looking at its closest neighbors.
The Team Builder: Combining many weak models to make one strong prediction.
The Fast Learner: A Microsoft algorithm known for being faster and more efficient than standard boosting.
The Categorical Expert: An algorithm designed specifically to handle categorical data (like 'Red', 'Blue') well.
The Anomaly Hunter: An algorithm used specifically to catch outliers (like fraud) by isolating them.
The Voting Booth: Training multiple models separately and averaging their answers.
The Error Fixer: Training models in a sequence, where each new model fixes the mistakes of the previous one.
The Manager: Training a 'meta-model' to learn how to combine the predictions of other models.
The Fair Draw: Picking a sample group that perfectly matches the demographics of the whole population.
The Translation Grader: A score used to rate how good a machine translation is compared to a human translation.
The Summary Grader: A score used to rate how good an AI summary is compared to a human one.
The Purity Test: A measurement used in Decision Trees to decide how to split the data best.
The Confidence Score: A metric that penalizes wrong answers more heavily if the AI was confident about them.
The Lowest Valley: The absolute best possible solution (lowest error) for a model.
The False Bottom: A point where the model thinks it found the best solution, but a better one exists elsewhere.
The Stuck Point: A flat spot in the training landscape where the model stops learning effectively.
The Single Number: A single value (like 5), as opposed to a vector or matrix.
The Dimensions: The number of directions a tensor has (Rank 0 is a scalar, Rank 1 is a vector).
The Hard-Easy Flip: The observation that high-level reasoning is easy for AI, but simple motor skills (walking) are hard.
The Understanding Debate: A thought experiment asking if a computer actually 'understands' language or just simulates it.
The Overwrite: When learning a new task causes the AI to completely forget the old task.
The AI Coach: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback—using one AI to train another AI.
The Rulebook: The policies ensuring data is secure, private, and accurate.
The Rust: The slow degradation of a model's performance as the real world changes over time.
The Brain Chip: A processor designed specifically to run neural networks efficiently.
The Custom Chip: A chip built for exactly one specific task (like mining Bitcoin or running AI).
The Rewritable Chip: A chip that can be reprogrammed after it is manufactured.
The Toolbox: A popular distribution of Python that comes with all the data science tools pre-installed.
The Studio: A popular professional code editor for Python developers.
The Corporate Lab: Google's secure, paid version of Colab for businesses.
The Open Assistant: An open-source alternative to ChatGPT provided by Hugging Face.
The Head Start: The benefit gained by the first company to enter a new market (like OpenAI with ChatGPT).
The Viral Value: When a service gets better the more people use it.
The Defense: A competitive advantage (like data or brand) that protects a company from rivals.
The Trap: When it's too expensive or difficult to switch from one cloud provider to another.
The Shared Brain: When a company releases the model's weights but not the training data or code.
The Black Box: Proprietary software where the code is kept secret.
The Countdown: How many months a startup has left before it runs out of cash.
The Spending Speed: How much money a startup spends per month.
The Cheat Sheet: A lookup table where the AI writes down the score for every possible move in every situation.
The Patience Score: A number that determines if the AI cares more about immediate rewards or long-term goals.
The Curiosity Dial: How often the AI tries a random move instead of the one it thinks is best.
The Cautious Learner: A training method that prevents the AI from making drastic, risky changes to its behavior.
The Memory Bank: Saving past games and re-watching them later to learn from mistakes.
The Loophole Finder: When an AI finds a way to get a high score without actually doing the task properly.
The Word Mapper: An early technique to turn words into numbers based on which words appear next to them.
The Global Counter: A method for understanding word meaning by counting how often words appear together in the whole library.
The Whispering Game: When the error signal gets too small to train the early layers of a deep network.
The Feedback Loop: When the error signal gets too huge and causes the model to crash.
The Self-Taught Master: An AI that learned Chess and Go from scratch just by playing against itself.
The Rule Breaker: An AI that learns to play games without even being told the rules first.
The Lite Racer: A vision model designed to be super fast and small, perfect for mobile phones.
The Balanced Scaler: A model architecture that perfectly balances speed, size, and accuracy.
The Wide Shortcut: An upgrade to ResNet that adds more parallel paths for data to flow through.
The Face Maker: A model famous for generating hyper-realistic human faces that don't exist.
The Style Swapper: Turning horses into zebras (and back) without needing matched pairs of photos.
The Enhancer: Using AI to turn a blurry, low-res photo into a sharp, high-res one.
The 3D Dreamer: Turning a set of 2D photos into a fully explorable 3D scene.
The Speed Tuner: NVIDIA's software that optimizes AI models to run super fast on their GPUs.
The AI Computer: A small, powerful computer by NVIDIA made specifically for robots and drones.
The Virtual Graduate: Training a robot in a video game (simulation) and then transferring that brain to a real physical robot.
The Subword Reader: A model that understands words by breaking them into parts (like 'run-ning').
The Context Switcher: A model that knows that 'bank' means something different in 'river bank' vs 'bank account'.
The Open Assistant: An open-source chatbot alternative to ChatGPT.
The Enterprise NLP: A company focused on building safe, private language models for businesses.
The Roleplayer: An AI platform designed specifically for chatting with fictional personas.
The Universal Adaptor: A file format that lets you move AI models between different coding tools.
The Robot Brain: The standard software 'plumbing' that lets different robot parts talk to each other.
The Robot Gym: A 3D simulator where robots can practice moving without breaking anything in the real world.
The Physics Engine: A super-accurate physics simulator used to train robots how to walk.
The Laser Eye: A sensor that shoots lasers to build a 3D map of the world, used by self-driving cars.
The Map Maker: 'Simultaneous Localization and Mapping' - a robot figuring out where it is while drawing a map at the same time.
The Arm Mover: Calculating exactly how to bend each robot joint to get the hand to a specific point.
The Flexibility Score: How many different ways a robot joint or arm can move.
The Muscle: The motor or piston that actually moves the robot's parts.
The Future Simulator: Simulating thousands of possible future games to decide the best move right now.
The Explorer: Giving the AI a 'reward' just for seeing something new, encouraging it to explore.
The Meaning Anchor: The problem of how an AI connects a word (like 'Apple') to the real-world object.
The Context Gap: The difficulty AI has in knowing which real-world facts change and which stay the same.
The Understanding Debate: A thought experiment asking if a computer actually 'understands' language or just simulates it.
The Trade-off Rule: The mathematical proof that no single algorithm is the best at every possible problem.
The Changing Rules: When the relationship between the data and the answer changes.
The Ingredient Pantry: A central place to store cleaned data features so different teams can reuse them.
The Hybrid Storage: Combining the best features of a Data Lake (raw files) and a Data Warehouse (structured tables).
The Unused Archives: Data that a company collects but never analyzes or uses.
The Billion Dollar Baby: A startup that reaches a valuation of $1 billion.
The Payday: The plan for selling the startup or going public.
The Self-Funding: Building a startup using only personal savings and revenue, without investor money.
The Fire Drill: Hiring hackers to attack your AI on purpose to find holes before the bad guys do.
The Security Guard: The team responsible for defending the AI against attacks in real-time.
The Reverse Engineer: A hack that recreates the private faces or data used to train an AI just by asking it questions.
The Privacy Leak: Figuring out if a specific person's data was used to train a model.
The Prison Break: Using clever prompts to trick the AI into bypassing its safety filters.
The Nutrition Label: A document that lists exactly what ingredients (data) are in the model and its side effects.
The Safety Manual: A document describing the safety checks and limitations of an AI system.
The Fairness Rule: Ensuring the AI gives the exact same percentage of positive outcomes to every group of people.
The Blur: Adding mathematical noise to data so general patterns are visible but individual people remain hidden.
The Piggy Bank: Saving up small updates over time to make one big change, allowing huge models to train on small chips.
The Hybrid Fuel: Using lower-quality numbers (16-bit) for speed and high-quality numbers (32-bit) for accuracy at the same time.
The Speed Limit: Preventing the model from changing too drastically in a single step to avoid crashing.
The Stretch: Starting training very slowly to let the model settle before speeding up.
The Cool Down: Slowly lowering the learning speed in a wave pattern to land on the best result.
The Mirror: Forcing two different parts of the model to use the exact same brain cells to save space.
The Training Wheels: Giving the model the correct answer during training instead of letting it guess, to help it learn faster.
The Shortcut: A math trick that makes the 'Attention' step in Transformers run much faster with less memory.
The Memory Manager: A technique used by vLLM to organize memory efficiently, like Tetris for GPU space.
The Direct Vote: Training an AI on human preferences directly without needing a separate 'judge' model.
The Thumbs Up: A simpler alternative to DPO that just asks 'was this output good or bad?' instead of comparing two.
The Compact Tuner: A technique that shrinks a model heavily (4-bit) and then fine-tunes it, allowing huge models to run on home PCs.
The Compass: A modern way of telling the model the order of words by rotating them in mathematical space.
The Extender: A trick that allows models to read longer documents than they were originally trained to handle.
The Short-Term Memory: Saving the math for previous words so the AI doesn't have to re-calculate them for every new word.
The Guesser: Having a small, fast model guess the next few words, and a big model just checking them (checking is faster than writing).
The Overlap Score: Measuring how perfectly a predicted box covers the actual object (used in object detection).
The Similarity Score: A statistic used to gauge the similarity of two samples, often used in medical image segmentation.
The Typo Counter: A standard metric for speech-to-text, counting how many words the AI heard wrong.
The Insight Score: How much of the 'weirdness' in the data can be explained by your model.
The Cluster Grade: Measuring how similar an object is to its own cluster (cohesion) compared to other clusters (separation).
The Sweet Spot Finder: A chart used to decide the perfect number of clusters (groups) for your data.
The Brain Split: Breaking a huge AI model into pieces and putting each piece on a different GPU.
The Homework Split: Giving the exact same model to multiple GPUs, but giving each one a different stack of homework (data) to grade.
The Assembly Line: Processing different layers of the model on different chips in a sequence.
The Math Split: Breaking a single giant matrix multiplication across multiple chips to do it instantly.
The Space Saver: A technique by DeepSpeed that removes duplicate data across GPUs so you can train bigger models.
The Overflow: Moving data from the fast GPU memory to the slower CPU memory when the GPU gets full.
The Super Bridge: A physical cable that lets NVIDIA GPUs talk to each other incredibly fast.
The Super Highway: A specialized network cable used in supercomputers to connect thousands of servers.
The Low-Res Math: Using extremely simplified numbers (8-bit) to do AI math twice as fast as usual.
The Firehose: Specialized memory on GPUs that can move data much faster than standard computer RAM.
The Window Watcher: A vision model that looks at images in shifting windows, making it great at seeing details.
The Object Spotter: A model that uses the Transformer architecture (usually for text) to find objects in images.
The Magic Scissors: A model by Meta that can cut out any object from an image with a single click.
The Image Prompter: A tool that lets you use an image as a prompt instead of words.
The Speed Painter: A version of Stable Diffusion that generates images in 4 steps instead of 20.
The Movie Maker: Applying diffusion techniques to generate consistent frames of video over time.
The 3D Scanner: Using AI to figure out what a 3D object looks like from a few 2D photos.
The Fast Chip: A new type of chip by Groq designed specifically to run LLMs instantly.
The AWS Trainer: A custom chip built by Amazon specifically for training AI models cheaply.
The AWS Runner: Amazon's custom chip designed just to run (infer) models at low cost.
The Intel Chip: Intel's specialized AI processor designed to compete with NVIDIA.
The Google Pod: The latest, most powerful version of Google's Tensor Processing Unit.
The Brain Chip: A computer chip physically built to mimic biological neurons and synapses (like Intel Loihi).
The Giant Chip: A chip the size of a dinner plate (by Cerebras) that holds an entire supercomputer on one piece of silicon.
The Light Speed: Using light (photons) instead of electricity to do AI math for higher speed and lower heat.
The Quantum Win: The point where a quantum computer solves a problem that a classical supercomputer cannot.
The Firehose: The speed at which data can be moved from the RAM to the processor. AI needs this more than raw speed.
The Molecule Mapper: The latest AI that predicts the structure of proteins, DNA, and RNA.
The Protein Folder: An open-source alternative to AlphaFold developed by the University of Washington.
The Medicine Hunter: Using AI to simulate how billions of chemicals interact with a virus to find a cure.
The DNA Reader: Using AI to read and interpret the genetic code of a human or virus.
The X-Ray Decoder: Extracting invisible data features from medical scans (CT, MRI) using AI.
The Second Opinion: AI systems that highlight suspicious areas in medical scans for doctors to check.
The Private Study: Training an AI on patient data across different hospitals without the data ever leaving the hospital.
The Digital Chart: The digital version of a patient's paper chart, analyzed by AI for trends.
The Fast Pandas: A new data library that is much faster than Pandas for massive datasets.
The Parallel Pandas: A tool that lets you run Pandas code across many computers at once.
The Scaler: A framework for scaling Python AI apps from one laptop to a massive cluster.
The Fast Math: A Google library that is like NumPy but runs on GPUs and is used for high-performance research.
The Flexible Net: A neural network library built on top of JAX, popular for research.
The Auto-Tuner: A library that automatically tests thousands of settings to find the best hyperparameters.
The Robot Sim: A physics engine used to train robots in a virtual world.
The Training Ground: The standard library for Reinforcement Learning environments (formerly OpenAI Gym).
The Web Scraper: A library used to pull data out of HTML and XML files.
The Browser Bot: A tool that automates web browsers, used to scrape dynamic websites.
The Variety Score: A metric for GANs that checks if the generated images look real and diverse.
The Realism Score: The standard way to measure how close generated images are to real images. Lower is better.
The Caption Grader: A metric used to judge how well an AI described an image compared to a human description.
The Translation Judge: A metric for machine translation that understands synonyms (e.g., 'fast' = 'quick').
The Correction Count: Measuring how many edits a human needs to make to fix the AI's translation.
The Perfect Score: A metric where the answer is counted as correct only if it is character-for-character identical.
The Privacy Law: Europe's strict law on how personal data can be collected and used.
The Transparency Rule: The legal right for a user to ask *why* an AI made a specific decision about them.
The Legal Armor: A promise by companies (like Microsoft) to pay your legal fees if you get sued for using their AI.
The Copy Exception: A legal doctrine allowing limited use of copyrighted material without permission (a big debate in AI training).
The Illegal Image: Non-consensual sexual imagery created by AI, now illegal in many places.
The Citizen Grade: Using AI to rate citizens based on behavior, banned in the EU AI Act.
The Face Tracker: Using AI to track people in public spaces using facial recognition, heavily restricted.
The Workhorse: The fast, cheap model that powers the free version of ChatGPT.
The Fast Assistant: Anthropic's cheaper, faster model for simple tasks.
The Heavyweight: The largest version of Meta's previous generation open-source model.
The Little Giant: A small model that outperforms much larger models thanks to smart training.
The Movie Maker: Stability AI's model for generating short video clips from images.
The Coder: The OpenAI model specifically trained on code that powers GitHub Copilot.
The Hallucination Art: An early computer vision program by Google that made psychedelic art by over-interpreting patterns.
The Failed Chatbot: Microsoft's 2016 chatbot that learned bad behavior from Twitter users and was shut down in 24 hours.
The Matrix Splitter: A math method used to simplify data and build recommendation systems.
The Recommender: Breaking a grid of users and ratings into smaller pieces to predict what movies you'll like.
The Newbie Issue: The difficulty of recommending things to a new user who hasn't clicked on anything yet.
The Shape Theory: The idea that high-dimensional data (like images) actually lies on a simple low-dimensional shape.
The Random Rule: The assumption that every data point is random and doesn't depend on the previous one.
The Random Roll: Running a simulation thousands of times with random numbers to predict an outcome.
The Margin Error: A loss function used specifically for SVMs (Support Vector Machines).
The Difference Score: Measuring how much one probability distribution differs from another.
The Line Data: A text file where every line is a separate JSON object, perfect for streaming massive AI datasets.
The Column File: A highly efficient storage format that is faster and smaller than CSV for big data.
The Row File: A data serialization system used in big data pipelines.
The Google Format: A binary file format used by TensorFlow to train models efficiently.
The Science File: A file format designed to store and organize large amounts of scientific data.
